attributes.stem:
An 80-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic accompanied by his daughter with concerns about functional decline over the past several months. She describes periods of confusion and disorientation interspersed with days where he is lucid and well-oriented. He reports seeing his young grandchildren playing in his home, though they live interstate. He experiences vivid nightmares and has had multiple falls in the past year. He has become incontinent of urine due to inability to reach the toilet in time. His medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and type 2 diabetes. His medications include metformin, perindopril, and atorvastatin.

On examination, he appears alert. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. He is fluent in speech and oriented to place and person but not time. He has no tremor. Tone is mildly increased in all limbs throughout the range of motion. Reflexes are normal with downgoing plantar responses. His gait is slow with short, shuffling strides and a narrow base. He scores 21/30 on a Mini Mental State Examination, performing particularly poorly on short-term memory tasks. His Frontal Assessment Battery is 14/18.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic tetrad of features consistent with Lewy body dementia: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism (bradykinesia, rigidity, and shuffling gait), and REM sleep behaviour disorder (manifesting as vivid nightmares). The visual hallucinations are characteristically detailed and recurrent. A highly specific feature is extreme sensitivity to antipsychotic medications, which can precipitate severe extrapyramidal symptoms or neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

Vascular dementia typically presents with stepwise deterioration rather than day-to-day fluctuations, and often features focal neurological deficits. The history lacks evidence of stroke or cerebrovascular events.

Normal pressure hydrocephalus can present with the triad of gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive impairment. However, the gait is typically wide-based rather than parkinsonian, and visual hallucinations are not a feature.

Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is a rapidly progressive prion disease characterised by dementia, myoclonus, and cerebellar signs. It typically progresses much more rapidly than this presentation, with an average survival of six months.

Corticobasal degeneration typically begins with asymmetric motor features, often affecting a single limb initially. The symmetrical presentation and prominent cognitive and psychiatric features make this diagnosis less likely.

Lewy body dementia is a neurodegenerative disease characterised by accumulation of alpha-synuclein, a feature shared with Parkinson's disease. This explains the overlap in clinical manifestations, particularly the parkinsonian features. While supportive radiological findings exist on MRI and SPECT/CT, these are not required for diagnosis.
learningPoints: Lewy body dementia is characterized by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, and REM sleep behavior disorder
Visual hallucinations in Lewy body dementia are typically detailed and recurrent
Extreme sensitivity to antipsychotic medications is a key feature of Lewy body dementia
references: Sanford A. Lewy Body Dementia. Clin Geriatr Med 2018; 34:603-15. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30336990/
Walker Z, Possin KL, Boeve BF, et al. Non-Alzheimer’s dementia 2: Lewy body dementias. Lancet 2015; 386:1683-97. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26595642/
